content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 31, 2017

Donna Mooney
Chief Assistant City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
555 Santa Clara Street
Vallejo CA 94590

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-17-010

Dear Ms. Mooney

        This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Vallejo City Councilmember
Pippin Dew-Costa regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

        Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of
interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

        May Councilmember Dew-Costa make, participate in making, or influence decisions on a
development application to build a cement facility in the city where the Councilmember acted as
real estate agent and received income for the sale of property located within 500 feet of the project
site?

CONCLUSION

        Since the Councilmember does not have an interest in the property located within 500 feet
of the proposed project nor the property owner and assuming no other undisclosed financial
interests of the Councilmember exist that may be affected by the project, it is not foreseeable that
the decisions will have a financial effect on the Councilmember or the Councilmember's interests.
Therefore, the Councilmember will not have a conflict of interest.

_____________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-010
Page No. 2

FACTS

The City of Vallejo City Council will be asked to consider in Spring 2017 whether to grant
land use entitlements including a conditional use permit and planned development permit, and
whether to certify an environmental impact report, sought by Orcem California, Inc. and Vallejo
Marine Terminal, LLC, to build a cement facility at 790 and 800 Derr Street, Vallejo.

Councilmember Pippin Dew-Costa is a realtor who represented Thomas Tener in the sale of
his single family home located at 117 Colt Court in November 2016. Councilmember Dew-Costa
does business under the brokerage RE/MAX Gold. The RE/MAX Gold office earned a total
commission of $8,062, which was paid by Mr. Tener. Councilmember Dew-Costa received a
portion of the commission, in the amount of $7,140. No referral or finder's fees were paid.

The distance between 117 Colt Court and the boundary of the proposed development is less
than 500 feet.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public
official's interests set forth in Section 87103. Section 87103 includes the following:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

• Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the personal
finances of a public official or his or her immediate family.

File No. A-17-010
Page No. 3

Your question concerns income received by the Councilmember when she acted as an agent
in a real estate sale on behalf of the seller of property.² Regulation 18700.1(c) provides a special
rule for the attribution of "commission income"³ received in a real estate transaction. Regulation
18700.1(c)(2)(C) provides:

"(2) The sources of commission income in a specific sale or similar
transaction include for each of the following:

* * *

"(C) A real estate agent:

"(i) The broker and brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
agent works;

"(ii) The person the agent represents in the transaction; and

"(iii) Any person who receives a finder's or other referral fee for referring
a party to the transaction to the broker, or who makes a referral pursuant to a
contract with the broker."

Thus, under your facts the potentially disqualifying sources of income to the
Councilmember would be RE/MAX (the broker/brokerage business entity under whose auspices the
Councilmember works) and Mr. Tener. Under the attribution rule in Regulation 18700.1(c)(2)(C),
the buyer of Mr. Tenet's property (and current owner) would not be a source of income.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Particular standards apply to determine foreseeability and materiality, depending on whether
interest are "explicitly" or "not explicitly" involved in a decision. (Regulation 18701(a) and (b).)
Under the facts presented, the decisions do not explicitly involve RE/MAX or Mr. Tener.
Therefore, the applicable foreseeability standard is whether the financial effect can be recognized as
a realistic possibility. (Regulation 18701(b).) Nothing in the facts suggest a realistic possibility that
the decisions will financially affect RE/MAX or Mr. Tener.

Since the Councilmember does not have an interest in the property or property owner
located within 500 feet of the proposed project and assuming no other undisclosed financial
interests of the Councilmember exists that may be affected by the project, it is not foreseeable that
the decision will have a financial effect on the Councilmember and the Councilmember will not
have a conflict of interest.

² Note that Regulation 18701.1 provides: "Possession of a real estate sales or brokerage license, or any other
professional license, without regard to the official's business activity or likely business activity, does not in itself make
a material financial effect on the official's economic interest reasonably foreseeable."

³ "Commission income" means gross payments received by a public official as a result of services rendered as
a broker, agent, or other salesperson for a specific sale or similar transaction. Commission income is received when it is
paid or credited.

File No. A-17-010
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

sections.question:
May Councilmember Dew-Costa make, participate in making, or influence decisions on a 
development application to build a cement facility in the city where the Councilmember acted as 
real estate agent and received income for the sale of property located within 500 feet of the project 
· site?
sections.conclusion:
Since the Councilmember does not have an interest in the property located within 500 feet 
of the proposed project nor the property owner and assuming no other undisclosed financial 
interests of the Councilmember exist that may be affected by the project, it is not foreseeable that 
the decisions will have a financial effect on the Councilmember or the Councilmember' s interests. 
Therefore, the Councilmember will not have a conflict of interest.
